case_name: Sharon K. Susie, an individual and Larry D. Susie v. Family Health Care of Siouxland, P.L.C. d/b/a Family Health Care of Siouxland Urgent Care and Sarah Harty

summary.brief: Sharon K. Susie, an individual and Larry D. Susie v. Family Health Care of Siouxland, P.L.C. d/b/a Family Health Care of Siouxland Urgent Care and Sarah Harty. Medical malpractice case involving cardiology.

evidence_index.text: Sharon Susie fell in her living room, injuring her right arm. Her arm was bruised and painful.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Approximately one week later, on September 29, 2012, she went to the urgent care clinic of Family Health Care of Siouxland and was treated by Sara Harty, a physician’s assistant.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Harty ordered an x-ray of Sharon’s arm, which revealed 'no fractures or dislocations' but there was 'moderate soft tissue swelling about the elbow joint dorsally.'
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Harty diagnosed Sharon with right proximal forearm pain, elbow pain, and a right elbow contusion.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The next day, Sharon’s adult son found her extremely ill.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: She was diagnosed with septic shock and kidney failure.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The biopsy of Sharon’s right arm showed she had necrotizing fasciitis, also known as a flesh-eating disease.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: To stop the progression of the life-threatening disease, doctors amputated Sharon’s right arm.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Schechter will also opine to a reasonable degree of medical probability regarding the treatability of Sharon Susie’s infection at the point of time she presented to the urgent care clinic on September 29, 2012.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Schechter

evidence_index.text: He is also expected to testify that had the infection been diagnosed on the day of her visit to the clinic, and treatment initiated immediately, the spread of the infection, more likely than not, could have been avoided.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Schechter

evidence_index.text: Because the plaintiffs failed to set forth specific facts showing a prima facie case of causation and lost chance of survival, we affirm the judgment of the district court.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The district court stated on the record as follows: Okay. It’s clear to me even -- and I know, Mr. Humphrey, you wanted to make sure I read all of your other physician stuff. I did that. I still believe and I find that there is no -- that you don’t have the necessary expert more likely than not causation evidence to get the claim to a jury.
evidence_index.speaker: District court

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Sharon Susie fell in her living room, injuring her right arm. Her arm was bruised and painful. The condition of her arm did not improve over the week.

focused_exam.arm: Bruised and painful, moderate soft tissue swelling about the elbow joint dorsally

reveal.decision_impact: Did not prompt further investigation or treatment

Amputation of right arm and toes performed

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient presented with arm pain and swelling
decision_points.prompt: What should be the next step in management after the X-ray results?

options.clinical_reasoning: Given the soft tissue swelling and potential for infection, antibiotics should be considered

options.description: Provide pain management and advise follow-up if no improvement
options.clinical_reasoning: Focused on pain management without addressing potential infection

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant provided pain management and advised follow-up
actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Focused on pain relief and advised follow-up if no improvement

expected_action_court.description: Antibiotics should have been prescribed given the risk of infection
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony suggested early intervention with antibiotics could have prevented progression

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to consider potential infection risk and prescribe antibiotics
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Antibiotics should have been administered to prevent progression of infection
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed necrotizing fasciitis, leading to septic shock and kidney failure
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Amputation of right arm and toes

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Earlier diagnosis could have prevented severe outcomes

No fractures or dislocations; soft tissue swelling noted

To stop progression of necrotizing fasciitis
